originals: Causation is a psychological tool of humans to understand the world independently of language, and it is one of the principles involved in the construction of the human mental model of reality (Neeleman and van de Koot, 2012).

paraphrase_3: As Neeleman and van de Koot (2012) explain, Causation functions as a psychological device through which humans grasp reality without dependence on language, constituting a fundamental principle in the formation of human mental frameworks for understanding the world.
paraphrase_4: The psychological apparatus of Causation allows humans to make sense of their environment independently from language and represents a core principle underlying the creation of human cognitive representations of reality (Neeleman and van de Koot, 2012).

originals: Causal reasoning is the process of relating two events, namely cause and its effect.

paraphrase_2: When we engage in causal reasoning, we establish relationships between two events: one that causes and another that represents the effect.
paraphrase_3: The practice of causal reasoning involves drawing connections between paired events, specifically identifying which serves as cause and which as effect.
paraphrase_4: By examining how two events relate to each other—one being the cause, the other the effect—we engage in what is called causal reasoning.

Causality is defined as the semantic relation between two events (e1, e2), namely causing event (cause, e1) and caused event (effect, e2).
An event may be a verb, whose arguments may be explicitly present or not (subject, verb, object), or a noun phrase.

The relation can be explicit or implicit, in case it is explicitly expressed, it is signalled by a lexical marker (l), which may be a verb, a preposition, an adverb or an expression such as as a result of, see Table 1.
